title: Play Stand
summary: A pretend-play store front: a boxy base with a chalkboard back and a sign-holding hutch on top. Light enough that a kid can't tip it.
description:
This is a play shop — a counter your kid stands behind, with back shelves for merchandise, a chalkboard panel for the day's specials, and a hutch on top that holds swappable signs. It reads as a big project but it's almost entirely 1-by lumber, butt joints, glue, and finish nails.

The one real design constraint is weight up high. Anything a toddler leans on has to not come down on them, so the signs are foam board, not plywood, and the whole thing gets feet or a wide base so it can't tip forward. Do not skip the tip-resistance — a top-heavy play stand is a hazard, not a toy.

The back shelves deliberately sit flush to the 1x2 rungs and stop short of the front edge of the sides, leaving a lip so small items don't roll off. Paint it in bright colors and prime the chalkboard panel with chalkboard paint before final assembly — it's far easier to coat flat.
timeLabel: 6–8 hrs plus paint and chalkboard-coat drying

tools.note: Pocket screws hide the joinery on the base; nails and glue also work.
Only if you round or shape the sign tops or hutch sides.
A circular saw and square handle every cut here.
Most of the assembly is glue and 1-1/4" finish nails.

materials.name: 1x6 board, 10 ft
materials.note: Base sides, top, and back shelves.

materials.name: 3/4" dowel, 30"
materials.note: Spans the hutch to hang buntings or fabric.

materials.name: Foam board
materials.note: The signs. Lightweight on purpose — a falling sign shouldn't hurt anyone.

materials.name: Craft paint or primer and topcoat

cutList.note: Round stock; thickness and width are the diameter.

steps.title: Build the base box
steps.body: Assemble the two sides and the top into a three-sided box with glue and finish nails. This is the counter of the play shop — keep it square so the back panel drops in cleanly later.

steps.title: Add the back rungs and shelves
steps.body: Fasten the 1x2 rungs across the back of the box, then land the 1x4 back shelves on them. The shelves sit flush to the rungs and stop short of the front edge of the 1x6 sides — that setback leaves a lip that keeps small toys from rolling off. Add a fourth rung flush with the top shelf backs to give the plywood panel something to grab.

steps.title: Mount the chalkboard panel
steps.body: Mark where the shelf backs land on the sides in pencil, then set the 1/4" plywood over them and transfer the marks onto the panel so you nail into solid wood, not into gaps. Glue and nail the panel to the shelf backs. Coat it with chalkboard paint before or right after mounting — flat is easier to paint.

steps.title: Make it tip-proof
steps.body: Add feet or a wider footprint so the stand can't pitch forward or backward when a child leans on the counter. If you build the hutch taller than shown, make these wider still. This is the safety-critical step — do not skip it.

steps.title: Assemble the hutch top
steps.body: Find the center of the base top and attach the short 1x3 there, then the two hutch uprights on either side, over-extending equally so the frame is symmetric. Land the hutch top rail and center piece into the notched ends. Work off a centerline the whole time or it'll come out lopsided.

steps.title: Sign holders, dowel, and signs
steps.body: Attach the 1x2 sign side holders and top rails to form open slots the signs slide into, then set the dowel across the hutch for hanging buntings or fabric. Cut the signs from foam board — cheap, colorful, and light enough that a dropped one won't hurt anyone. Prime and paint the whole stand in bright colors.
materials: Craft paint or primer and topcoat

images.alt: Wooden pretend-play store stand with chalkboard back and a sign hutch on top
